evidence: Usage limits allow configuring monthly total traces and extended retention traces limits with rate limits returning HTTP 429; but these are billing/throughput caps, not agent budget caps or closed-loop guardrails.

Reviewers can edit runs to create corrected examples and write assertions; annotation queues provide human review workflows, but these govern evaluation data rather than live agent action approval or kill switches.

Online evaluations track quality continuously on live traffic with anomaly detection; evaluations can run via rules and standard testing tools like pytest/Vitest, with regression tests asserting new versions must outperform baseline metrics.
Annotation Queues let users review and rate traces manually and leave notes, but this is evaluation labeling rather than oversight of live agent actions; no approval gate or kill switch described.
Experiments support comparing prompt/model/code changes with CI/CD integration for blocking deployments on regressions, plus online production scoring and semantic cost/usage observability.
Evaluators run with "automatic rate limiting, error management, and dynamic concurrency handling" — adjacent to bounded/budget control but for evals, not agent guardrails.

Server-side evaluators attach to datasets and "Phoenix scores experiment results automatically"; experiments determine "whether an update is an improvement or a regression" — evals as repeatable test cases in the dev/CI loop.

GitHub Actions eval-action runs evaluations on every pull request; --first N smoke runs on PRs and full runs on merges to catch regressions before production; online scoring of live traffic and semantic dashboards (latency, token usage, costs, scores).

AI Guard is designed to inspect, block, and govern AI behavior in real time, operating inline with the app/agent with Tool Protection securing tool/action execution against the agentic lethal trifecta.

AI Guard is designed to inspect, block, and govern AI behavior in real time inline with the execution path, blocking consequential actions; but no configurable approval gate/kill-switch/tiered autonomy is detailed.

Evaluations (LLM-as-a-judge, managed, external via API) configured with Write permissions plus Export API; semantic observability via end-to-end tracing and out-of-the-box cost/latency golden-signal dashboards.

Distributed tracing captures every step with trace enrichment/metadata, providing audit trail-like provenance; human annotation via Review Mode/Annotation Queues is evaluation labeling, not live action oversight.

GitHub integration to 'automatically run experiments on code changes and set performance thresholds' and 'Trigger automated experiment runs on code changes' with alerts on degradation (evals in CI/CD); plus alerts and semantic observability via traces.
The @tool decorator supports cost tracking and a @guardrail decorator is listed among available decorators, but without described configurable behavior.

Session recording captures execution times, LLM calls, events, errors and timing breakdowns visualized on a waterfall timeline (observability signals), but no CI/CD evals or rollout mechanisms described.

Guardrails 'actively moderates and mitigates harmful content in both prompts and responses' via real-time moderation, an adjacent closed-loop guardrail but without configurable interrupt/budget/iteration mechanisms.

Captures 'agent handoffs—how often agents delegate to other agents' and 'error propagation: how failures cascade through agent workflows,' monitoring inter-agent coupling but without governing/detecting emergent population behavior.

Alerting with warning/critical thresholds, notification channels, and 'alert history and audit logs' provides monitoring/audit trails but no approval gates, override, or kill switches over agent actions.

Experiments support regression testing to 'protect against quality degradation as you develop' through standard test suites before deployments, plus semantic observability via 14+ enrichment metrics and golden-signal alerts.

Apply guardrails, run evaluations, and monitor quality and compliance in real time across all agents; traces capture guardrails and retry/fallback logic, but no configurable budget caps/iteration limits detailed.
Multi-agent runs render as 'agent graph' structures showing delegation patterns; traces the multi-agent workflow but does not describe governing inter-agent coupling.
Approve, correct, or guide agent actions with a clear control panel; build workflows that keep humans in the loop for safety, accuracy, or sensitive decisions.
Framework instrumentation using OpenTelemetry with @traced spans, Total Cost/Total Tokens telemetry, and run evaluations with guardrails in the runtime.

Input/Output Guards intercept LLM inputs/outputs to detect, quantify and mitigate risks, with on_fail actions (exception, fix, reask, filter, refrain, noop) — a closed-loop guardrail, though not budget/iteration bounded.

Execution rails 'control and validate tool/function calls, their arguments, and results to safely interact with external systems'; input/output rails block or modify content based on defined policies.

Output rails filter, edit, or block unsafe content before it reaches users, and execution rails validate tool calls; approval/blocking gates on actions but no explicit human escalation/override described.

OpenTelemetry distributed tracing to monitor guardrails execution, track activated rails, monitor LLM performance, plus evaluation/measurement across rails; policy-as-code via YAML configs but no CI/CD evals or rollout described.

Agent Behavior Defense controls tool usage through an 'Off-Task Action detector' and 'Tool Allow/Deny List,' enforcing least-privilege tool access; guardrails block prompt injections and harmful content in a closed loop.

Guardrails detect and stop harmful/unwanted content and audit logs let admins 'investigate and respond to threats'; RBAC and logging described, but no explicit approval gate, kill switch, or escalation workflow.

Policies Overview and configurable guardrail thresholds plus SIEM log export to S3 and latency/analytics golden signals; agent-security evaluation frameworks exist but no described CI/CD evals or progressive rollout.
Budget caps ('Maximum spend in USD ($1000/month)'), rate limits, model allowlists/denylists for least-privilege tool access, and real-time guardrails that block requests (446/deny setting).

OpenTelemetry-compliant observability logs and records all requests/responses and supports tracing throughout request lifecycle, providing audit-trail-like logging; but no approval gates/kill switches described.

OpenTelemetry-compliant observability with 21+ metrics (cost, latency, accuracy) and 'Send Feedback' to build evaluation datasets; semantic observability adjacent but no CI/CD evals or progressive rollout described.
max_budget parameter, tpm_limit/rpm_limit rate limits, max_parallel_requests, and models array for allowed model access (least-privilege), plus pre-call guardrails that filter/block requests.
Keys can be disabled with /key/block and re-enabled with /key/unblock; spend logs record API key hash, user, team ID for audit-style tracking.
Per-key virtual identities and spend tracking with logging callbacks (Langfuse, MLflow) and per-key/user/team observability, but no evals-in-CI or progressive rollout described.

Guardrails is deployed between your application and MCP servers/LLM provider, raising rules to prevent malicious behavior; and scans require explicit user consent before starting each server (least-privilege/gating), but no configurable budget/iteration limits described.

Rules detect 'flows between tools' using `(call: ToolCall) -> (call2: ToolCall)` and detect 'Toxic Flows' and 'Tool Shadowing' across sequential tool calls, but this is single-agent tool-flow monitoring rather than inter-agent coupling governance.

Guardrails raise/block malicious agent behavior via rules deployed inline for 'continuous steering and monitoring', and scans require explicit user consent before starting each server, but no described approval gate/kill-switch/audit-provenance for live agent decisions.

Policy-like rule-based guardrails via 'simple Python-inspired matching rules' evaluated automatically on each LLM/MCP request through the Gateway, resembling policy-as-code gating, though not tied to CI/CD or rollout.
Guardrails validate and filter content at key points; PII detection with block strategy raises exception; before-agent hooks for rate limiting and blocking requests
HITL middleware pauses before executing sensitive tool calls with approve/edit/reject/respond decisions gated by policy and 'when' predicates
Function-based and LLM-based guardrails validate task outputs, with `guardrail_max_retries` (default 3) controlling retry attempts before failure; also `max_rpm` caps requests per minute.
`human_input=True` prompts users for verification/approval before agents finalize output; `@human_feedback` decorator pauses flow execution to collect human feedback.

Observability guidance recommends instrumenting code, configuring dashboards, establishing alerts, tracking token usage/costs and safety compliance across nine platforms plus Patronus AI for evaluation.

Termination conditions include TokenUsageTermination (stops when token budget hit), MaxMessageTermination (iteration limit), TimeoutTermination, plus least-privilege via specifying handoffs and disabling parallel tool calls.
UserProxyAgent provides human approval during runs (approve/disapprove with button click), ExternalTermination allows external kill of the run, and HandoffMessage escalates to a human user.
Native OpenTelemetry support collects telemetry with create_agent, invoke_agent, and execute_tool spans, giving traced observability but not full semantic observability or CI/CD evals.
Retry Policy Maximum Attempts specifies the maximum number of execution attempts, and timeouts (Start-To-Close, Schedule-To-Close) bound activity duration despite failures.
Human-in-the-loop interactions like validating LLM results or approving agent decisions, facilitated via Signals/Updates message passing.
Inspect and troubleshoot an AI application's performance, inputs, and outputs from a detailed UI; Event History records series of commands/events as source of truth.
Tool guardrails wrap function tools and validate before/after execution; tripwires halt execution immediately, and needs_approval enables least-privilege per-call tool gating.
HITL flow pauses agent execution until a person approves or rejects sensitive tool calls via state.approve()/state.reject() on RunState interruptions.

Built-in tracing captures spans/traces with metadata and integrates with observability platforms (Datadog, Langfuse, etc.), plus guardrail evaluation spans, but no policy-as-code/CI eval gates or rollout described.

Approval gating deterministically blocks high-stakes function calls, with stakes classification (low/medium/high) and 'escalations and timeouts (Promise.race / Promise.all semantics)' — but no configurable budget caps or iteration limits described.

The `@hl.require_approval()` decorator blocks specific function calls until a human has been consulted; upon denial feedback is passed to the LLM, with granular routing to specific users/teams across Slack/Email/Discord.

Run IDs and Call IDs track approval/human-contact requests and correlate them with agent runs; enterprise Audit Logs, agent/response webhooks, and learning & auto-approvals from prior decisions are mentioned but without detailed operational configuration.

Guardrails define intervention points (user input, tool call, tool response, output) with 'Annotate and block' action for agents, plus least-privilege RBAC-scoped agent identity tool access with narrow scopes.

Guardrails block risky content at tool-call/output intervention points; blueprint governance can disable or revoke permissions for all agents of a kind; agents get independent audit trails and inventory/auditing through Entra Agent ID.

Build-test-deploy-monitor lifecycle with evaluations to catch regressions, per-agent Entra workload identity, versioning with rollback to previous versions, and end-to-end tracing/metrics observability.
